item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea I'd gotten was that SCP-2141-1 isn't host to SCP-2141's offspring yet, she's still being metamorphosed into something more akin to SCP-2141 so that she can host them.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first and fourth images that were used in this article could not be sourced, and thus are assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). The second image is from here and the third image is from AP as indicated in this article. Thus, all four images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definite upvote. I do wonder though, what about it's offspring? What is the Foundation going to do about them? The interview was a touch of gold, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really appreciated the "HR" aspect of the interview log. Showing how the Foundation learns and corrects course through the careers of individuals is not common, and felt really well done in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A number of interesting ideas, executed brilliantly. I honestly like the nesting probe theory of the wyrm/human relationship better than any theory that 2141 uses -1 in place of an actual reproductive partner- the former is a much more novel explanation for why so many monsters are so interested in kidnapping human ladies.

The introduction of the Bible parallel was very well done- so subtle you wouldn't notice it, until you get partway through the note and the facts suddenly all fit together in a different shape than before.

The interview log doesn't add much to the concept of 2141, but I still really enjoyed it. The Site Director obviously had no way of guessing that her course of action wasn't the best one for the circumstances, and I was braced for the Foundation to punish her anyway (my experience says, if you ever have to explain yourself inside an SCP article, You're Gonna Have A Bad Time)… and then she made some excellent points about the situation, provided some actionable intelligence, and pointed out that the people judging her probably weren't entirely objective (honestly just raising the tension, "bad things happen to good people" is the theme of about half this site)… and then it turned out okay! An extraordinarily pleasant surprise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This is quite underrated.

One thing I like about this is that it uses a lot of pictures to help with pacing. Some of the more recent articles made me lose interest mid-way because the whole thing is just paragraphs after paragraphs of boring text. Adding pictures (or hell, even just messing with the format a little bit) refreshens the reader's interest and allows them to carry on reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I appreciate your feedback. This article was much longer in its initial draft so cutting it down and maintaining coherency was a challenge. I can see how that might have meant that it became a little incoherent in the final draft.

As for your confusion, initially this article explicitly referred to SCP-2141 as an earthworm-like creature (and I think I might put that back in). Earthworms are hermaphrodites, but they can't asexually reproduce even though they can regenerate. The need to make contact with another worm in order to reproduce. The idea here is that this worm uses the female host to replace another worm of its size (because none is readily available).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A big worm, in a small tunnel with a small worm as a spore that its protective of…. I think I may have missed something but a lot of the information seems superfluous. Actually I'm not sure what I've read or missed but I'm completely perplexed about this one. Big ass worm in a small ass tunnel…. Is that the scary part? That they could be under almost anywhere?
Having the 2142-1 leaving and having to be collected seems an extremely weird set of behavior as well. Why wouldnt the worm simply reproduce a smaller version of itself? Or is the big worm male and needs a female host?
The article was a good length but at the end of it I looked back over all the text I'd read and wondered how much of it I could cut and still have it make sense. That was when I realised I had no idea what I'd just read and read it again. I think I'm missing something :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This is the way people talk in real life. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed this issue with a phrase that I thought the word "official" implied, but fair enough.

Edit made to clarify that the Foundation wasn't just chillin' until the family arrived.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two (2) field agents posing as Forest Service rangers were sent to the residence of Mr. and Mrs. █████████ upon their arrival to extract official permission to survey the tunnel under the guise of removing hazardous material and dangerous local fauna.

Foundation respecting private property? i LoL'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was supposed to imply that for the purposes of the interview they're not using names, but I also wasn't averse to people reading beyond that. Glad you enjoyed the article. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the article overall until the interview, the interview itself sold me completely. Its nice to not only see foundation personnel acting like normal people, but also the repercussions of fucking up containment procedures. Easy +1 and I hope to see more articles from you.

I called the O5s, – (pauses) – “twenty-eight,” not you.

Are that pause and quotations marks implying something personal, or am I reading too much into it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember your original draft of this, and holy fuck was it nowhere near as good as this. The buildup's handled well, the pictures are great and the dialogue is good, plus the body horror aspects work. I wish I'd looked at this when it was unposted, so I could have told you how good it was then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a big, big fan of slice-of-life Foundation stuff, and a supervisor talking to his subordinate in a really human way makes me infinitely more involved in the reading. I've seen you shopping this in chat, and I'm glad to see that you got something nice out of it. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dig it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ew, fixing that now then. Thanks for the kind words. :)

As people remind me that they read this over and provided feedback I'll of course mention them, it's hard to remember given the multiple times I went over this to condense it from its original state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I reviewed this in the chats, and holy cow it has infinitely improved since I read it then, and I liked it before. +1

Having said that, one extremely minor things I noticed was :"…consisting of one seasonal vacation home seasonally occupied by a married couple of one (1) year…" You said "seasonal" and "seasonally" in the same sentence. Stuff like that drives me completely insane.

After re-reading the interview log, I'm glad you didn't change it too much; it sounds more natural and reveals (in an indirect way) more information about the scip.

All in all, absolutely fantastic. SCP-2141-1 thoroughly creeped me out, and that's what I like to see in a scip. Have a well-earned upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read that, it's great, you should still finish it.

Anyway, this SCP is unsettling in a good way and as Mailboss wrote above, goes way beyond its "death worm" cliché origins. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. I had a draft somewhat similar to this, but located beneath the Balkan Peninsula.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! SophiaLight helped me with some feedback on my first draft in the forums, AndarielHalo, Crealis as well as some others gave me reactions in chat.

Editing this as I remember who helped me, this was edited over a couple of review sessions.

First SCP I've put on the mainlist, so we'll see how it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic. I was skeptical at first, sensing a cliche giant worm, but it got really good.
Awesome job on this!
